questions.prompt: Under the EU AI Act, what are the prohibited AI practices and what makes them different from high-risk systems in terms of a compliance route?
must_include: Article 5 enumerates the prohibited practices (e.g. social scoring, untargeted facial-recognition scraping, real-time RBIS in public spaces for law enforcement)
there is no conformity-assessment route around them; they are an absolute / unconditional ban
questions.provenance: EU AI Act Reg 2024/1689 ch02 (Article 5 prohibited practices)
must_not_include: a provider can place a prohibited-practice system on the market after passing a third-party conformity assessment

questions.prompt: Explain the two routes by which an AI system becomes high-risk under Article 6, and the de minimis carve-out that can exclude an Annex III system.
must_include: Route A: safety component of an Annex I product that requires third-party conformity assessment
Route B: the system falls within an Annex III use-case area
the Art 6(3) de minimis carve-out (narrow procedural task, improving a completed human activity, detecting patterns, or a preparatory task), but profiling of natural persons always makes it high-risk
questions.provenance: EU AI Act Reg 2024/1689 ch03 (Article 6 two-route classification, Art 6(3) carve-out)
must_not_include: a system that performs profiling of natural persons can still qualify for the de minimis carve-out and avoid high-risk classification

questions.prompt: A company sells an AI tool that screens and ranks CVs to filter job applicants during recruitment. Is this high-risk under the EU AI Act, and why?
must_include: it falls within Annex III Area 4 (employment / recruitment / filtering job applications) via Route B of Article 6
high-risk classification triggers the full Chapter III requirements (e.g. risk management, data governance, human oversight) unless a de minimis carve-out is documented
questions.provenance: EU AI Act Reg 2024/1689 ch12 (Annex III Area 4 employment) and ch03 (Art 6 Route B)
must_not_include: CV-screening recruitment AI is a prohibited practice under Article 5

questions.prompt: List the requirements imposed on high-risk AI systems under Articles 8-15 and note how they must be maintained.
must_include: risk management system (Art 9), data governance (Art 10), technical documentation (Art 11), automatic logging / record-keeping (Art 12)
transparency to deployers (Art 13), human oversight (Art 14), accuracy/robustness/cybersecurity (Art 15)
the requirements apply cumulatively and must be maintained throughout the lifecycle, not just before placement
questions.provenance: EU AI Act Reg 2024/1689 ch04 (Articles 8-15 high-risk requirements)
must_not_include: satisfying six of the seven high-risk requirements is sufficient for compliance

questions.prompt: A startup trains a general-purpose AI model and releases it under a free open-source licence with public weights. Its cumulative training compute exceeds 10^25 FLOPs. What GPAI obligations apply, and does the open-source exception help?
must_include: exceeding 10^25 FLOPs creates a rebuttable presumption of systemic risk
the open-source exception (Art 53(2)) does NOT apply to systemic-risk GPAI models, so the documentation and downstream-information obligations still apply
systemic-risk providers additionally owe adversarial testing / red-teaming, systemic risk assessment and mitigation, serious incident reporting to the AI Office, and cybersecurity
questions.provenance: EU AI Act Reg 2024/1689 ch08 (Chapter V GPAI; Art 51 10^25 FLOPs threshold; Art 53(2) open-source exception; Art 55)
must_not_include: open-source GPAI models with systemic risk are exempt from all Chapter V obligations because the weights are public

questions.prompt: Describe the three national penalty tiers under Article 99 and which violation each maps to.
must_include: up to EUR 35 million or 7% of global annual turnover for prohibited practices (Art 5)
up to EUR 15 million or 3% for operator-obligation and transparency violations
up to EUR 7.5 million or 1% for supplying incorrect, incomplete, or misleading information; fines are calculated on total worldwide annual turnover
questions.provenance: EU AI Act Reg 2024/1689 ch11 (Article 99 three-tier penalty structure)
must_not_include: the penalty percentages are applied only to the operator's EU-derived revenue rather than total worldwide turnover

questions.prompt: How does the US NIST AI Risk Management Framework define its four core functions?
We are placing an AI creditworthiness-scoring system on the EU market. Which obligations apply to us as the provider and which conformity steps do we need before launch?
